title: Single-Slit Diffraction Demonstrator
tagline: Visualize wave interference with a laser and adjustable slit
description: A tabletop apparatus that lets students adjust slit width and observe how the diffraction pattern changes on a projection screen. Built from laser-cut acrylic and a cheap laser pointer, this is a crowd-pleasing demo that makes abstract wave optics tangible.

title: CD Grating Spectrometer
tagline: Turn a smartphone and a CD into a lab-grade spectrometer
description: A cardboard and CD spectrometer that connects to a smartphone camera. Students can capture emission spectra from fluorescent lights, LEDs, and flames. Includes a calibration guide and companion worksheet for measuring wavelengths.

title: Newtonian Telescope Kit
tagline: A fully functional 4.5" reflector built from machined parts
description: A modular Newtonian reflector with a 4.5-inch parabolic mirror. All structural parts are designed for CNC routing and 3D printing. Includes optical alignment guides, eyepiece adapter designs, and curriculum for a three-session telescope-building workshop.

title: Schlieren Imaging Setup
tagline: Make heat and airflow visible with mirrors and a point light source
description: Schlieren imaging reveals invisible refractive index gradients in air — flames, breath, and turbulence become visible. This single-mirror design uses a concave mirror, a pinhole, and a phone camera. Stunning for demonstrations and deeply educational about light-matter interaction.

title: Polarization Exploration Kit
tagline: Six hands-on demos with polarizing film and everyday materials
description: A kit built around sheets of polarizing film, a white LED backlight panel, and a collection of birefringent materials (tape, cellophane, mica). Guides six modular demos from Malus's Law to stress visualization in plastic. Designed for concurrent use at multiple stations.

title: Total Internal Reflection Fiber Demo
tagline: Bend light through a water stream and a plastic fiber bundle
description: Two complementary demos: a laminar water stream that guides a laser by TIR, and a DIY plastic optical fiber bundle that transmits images. Together they build intuition for how fiber-optic communication works. Suitable for any age group from middle school to undergrad.
